title: Cleome linophylla
intro: Cleome linophylla, also known as Mexican poppy mallow or spider flower, is an annual herbaceous plant native to Mexico and the southwestern United States, belonging to the Cleomaceae family. The plant has been used in traditional medicine for its potential health benefits, including anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antimicrobial properties, which may help alleviate various health conditions such as fever, respiratory issues, and skin infections. The bioactive constituents of Cleome linophylla include alkaloids, flavonoids, and saponins, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. Cleome linophylla is commonly used to make herbal preparations such as teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are used to treat a range of health issues, including fever, cough, and skin conditions.
taxonomy: Cleome linophylla is classified under the family Cleomaceae, commonly known as the spiderflower or spider flower family. This classification places it within the order Brassicales and the clade rosids. The order Brassicales is a group of flowering plants that also includes mustard, cabbage, and broccoli. The Cleomaceae family is a small family of flowering plants native to tropical and subtropical regions. Cleome linophylla's classification provides insight into its evolutionary relationships and shared characteristics with other plants in its family.
